part: A Family That Voted in Haywood County
title: Three Counties Where Blacks Did Not Vote
topic: A Family's Push for the Vote
summary: Interviewed with her husband present, Mildred Bond Roxborough opens on her childhood in Haywood County, Tennessee, one of three counties in the state where Black citizens could not vote, and recounts how her parents campaigned to register and organized the Black majority who outnumbered whites three to one, in Brownsville, the county seat where she was born.

title: Get Walter White to Send Us a Charter
summary: Roxborough describes her parents organizing the first NAACP branch in Brownsville, needing people who could withstand the anticipated pressure, and writing away for a charter from executive secretary Walter White, and recalls being about seven and reading The Crisis magazine her parents subscribed to.

title: Can I Write to Roy Wilkins?
summary: She recounts learning of The Crisis through her mother reading Langston Hughes aloud, and at nine writing editor Roy Wilkins herself to ask permission to sell subscriptions, treasuring the signed reply she received from the editor.

title: Take Care of Your Paw, He's Been Hurt
summary: Roxborough tells how by 1939 her father was branch president and her mother secretary, how he was repeatedly arrested, and how law officers dumped him badly beaten on the couch while she was home alone at nine, saying he would be in jail if he lacked connections.

title: Only the Howard Theater Welcomed Us
topic: From Dr. Severe to Segregated Washington
summary: Her mother explains that the white county coroner Dr. Severe, her grandmother's half brother, likely interceded to keep her father out of jail; then, recalling her wartime years at Howard from 1943 to 1945 and later NYU's Washington Square campus, Roxborough describes attending the Howard Theater because other theaters admitted Black patrons only on a segregated basis, students testing restaurants, and the uniformed servicemen in the federal training program.

title: Why Are You Doing That to That Girl?
summary: Roxborough recounts joining the NAACP staff in the fall of 1953 to run a six-week membership campaign for Gloster B. Current in Baltimore, Thurgood Marshall laughing that Current should be ashamed for sending her to the demanding Dr. Lillie M. Jackson, and surviving the experience with Jackson's daughter Juanita Jackson Mitchell and the support of Henry G. Parks Jr. and the gambler known as Little Willie Adams.

title: I Never Heard So Many Cuss Words
summary: She gives her impressions of Thurgood Marshall as a profane, humorous raconteur who could make difficult things sound easy and talked her into Hoxie, Arkansas, and of Roy Wilkins as an elegant, unpretentious purist who rode the nickel subway, refused bodyguards, and demanded staff dedicate themselves to the work and present the NAACP as a serious organization.

title: He Could Live in Two Worlds
summary: Roxborough describes Walter White as a sociable man who loved the spotlight and, able to pass as Caucasian, investigated lynchings in Mississippi, Georgia, and Arkansas without being assaulted, then turns to Daisy Bates of Little Rock, a stylish, talkative people-person who drew barely literate members into the NAACP during the period leading to the integration of Central High under segregationist governor Orval Faubus.

title: Quietly Militant
summary: She remembers staying in Rosa Parks's Montgomery home before Parks left Alabama, describing Parks as a charming, laconic branch secretary, quietly militant with her husband and focused on organizing youth, and recalls E.D. Nixon as a dedicated figure who refused to surrender the Alabama membership rolls to the attorney general before the NAACP was banned in the state.

title: Mississippi Going Out of Bounds
summary: Roxborough recalls the office reaction to Emmett Till's murder, coming just after the killing of branch president Gus Courts amid the voter-registration push, the sense that nothing was sacred in Mississippi, and arranging a tour of branches for Till's mother, Mamie Till Bradley, who grew more militant and insisted on the open casket at the Chicago funeral.

title: If She's Here, She'll Help Me
summary: Roxborough tells how, while executive assistant to Roy Wilkins, she was called to the front office to meet a man who turned out to be Clarence Norris, a fugitive Scottsboro defendant, who asked for the office manager Bobbie Branch by name; Branch welcomed him and arranged for him to meet Dr. John Morsell.

title: I Want to Clear My Name Before I Die
summary: She explains that after the Communist Party's earlier involvement had led the NAACP to step aside, Norris returned asking first for money and then to clear his name, and how General Counsel Nathaniel R. Jones assigned attorney James I. Meyerson, leading Meyerson, Wilkins, Jones, and Norris to Alabama to secure a full pardon from the governor.

title: Every Five Years a New Job
summary: After noting the Scottsboro story produced the book The Last of the Scottsboro Boys, Roxborough clarifies she was never director of education (June Shagaloff was) but held many roles, and describes the ACT-SO academic, cultural, scientific Olympics founded by Benjamin L. Hooks and Vernon Jarrett to develop youth achievement across twenty-four fields through local, regional, and national competitions, a program grown to some 600 units with the Metropolitan New York chapter the largest.

title: Wilkins in Elegant Prose, Hooks in the Gospel Style
summary: She recounts Roy Wilkins succeeding Walter White, who died suddenly in 1955 while on leave after Wilkins had served as acting executive, and contrasts the intellectual Wilkins, who laid out facts in elegant prose, with his successor Benjamin L. Hooks, a dramatic Baptist minister who used a gospel approach to inspire constituents and keep the branches engaged.

part: What the NAACP Won and Forgot
title: We Forget Our History
summary: Noting the roughly 1700 branches and 500 youth and college units and asked why the NAACP's achievements are not better known after a century, Roxborough says people do not focus on their history and take for granted hard-won gains like school desegregation, public accommodations, and Boynton v. Virginia, forgetting the organization that was often the only instrument able to achieve them.

title: All of Us Could Sit at That Counter
summary: Roxborough credits the SCLC for bringing religious leaders and their congregations into the struggle and moving flexibly community to community, and praises the student sit-ins of SNCC for showing older people a simple act anyone could do, walking into a lunch counter and refusing to move, which inspired adults to demonstrate.

title: It Veered Toward Separatism
summary: She describes CORE as a more intellectual, racially integrated group under James Farmer, a former NAACP program director, that drew younger members and encouraged white participation, and judges the Black Panther Party as a militant group with energy that she believed misdirected itself toward Black separatism she considered self-defeating.

title: Done Without Any Legal Action
summary: Rejecting the idea that the NAACP changed things only through the courts, Roxborough cites Executive Order 8802, won through the association's lobbying and the relationship among Walter White, Eleanor Roosevelt, and A. Philip Randolph, and the first Civil Rights Law passed through the Washington Bureau's lobbying rather than litigation.

title: Thirty-Six Times Before the Supreme Court
summary: She suggests the NAACP is seen as primarily a legal body because its lawyers appeared so often before the Supreme Court, General Counsel Robert Carter making some thirty-six appearances and winning about thirty, and Thurgood Marshall arguing more than two dozen, even though grassroots lobbying was equally essential to the progress won.

title: Discipline Could Be Achieved in Leadership
summary: Roxborough offers her opinion that Malcolm X showed discipline could be achieved in leadership, drawing former street people into a cohesive group, that his death seriously affected the community, and that especially among NAACP youth there were those who admired his discipline and conviction even as he modified his early near-anarchic rhetoric to pressure the power structure.

part: A Lawyer Husband and the Detroit Branch
title: The Lead Lawyer in Those Cases
summary: Roxborough describes her husband John, who chaired the Legal Redress Committee of the Detroit branch and was lead lawyer in housing and education cases won before the Michigan Supreme Court, work she encountered while organizing the branch's first Freedom Fund life membership dinner with Arthur L. Johnson, and which earned commendations from Thurgood Marshall's national office.

title: It's the Leadership
summary: Reflecting that she and John shared a civil rights interest in their early days, Roxborough answers what separates an effective branch from a lackluster one: leadership, the extent to which leaders inspire the board and members determines how creative and forceful the branch becomes.

part: Holding the Line in the Deep South
title: We Are Not Going to Give Those Lists Up
summary: Roxborough recounts the defiant national-office response when Alabama and another state demanded the membership rolls, and how Ruby Hurley, who had organized southeastern units from Birmingham before the ban moved her office to Atlanta, returned with volunteers to work under cover of night, her life repeatedly threatened, getting people to register and continue the fight.

title: Send People to Fill the Void
summary: She describes the New York office sending people into Mississippi after Medgar Evers's assassination to reassure constituents the NAACP was not intimidated, with Evers's brother Charles taking over as field director and later becoming a Mississippi mayor.

title: That's My Shotgun You Have Your Feet On
summary: Roxborough recounts being sent to Mississippi in the 1960s to interview Delta farmers denied winter credit as economic reprisal for NAACP activity, riding with field director Medgar Evers, discovering her bare feet resting on his shotgun one night, and driving back each evening to a motel in the Black town of Mound Bayou amid two recent branch-president killings.

part: Little Rock and the Women of the Movement
title: Carefully Tended for Their Security
summary: Roxborough recalls meeting the Little Rock students as a carefully guarded group at gatherings Daisy Bates arranged before Central High's integration, and remembers most the quiet determination of Ernest Green, who later joined the NAACP board, and the strong personality of Minnijean Brown, while the others moved forward quietly wanting to finish school.

title: You Didn't Have Many Women Beyond Support Levels
topic: Ella Baker and the Early Women
summary: Roxborough says she knew Septima Clark and Bernice Robinson only by reputation, recalls the forceful Ella Baker, who worked in the Department of Branches and helped found SNCC, and notes how few women rose beyond support roles in those years, citing Lucille Black, who began as a secretary at seventeen and became membership secretary, and Bobbie Branch, who rose from clerk to office manager.

title: No Eye Was Not Dotted
summary: She remembers Jack Greenberg as a young lawyer working alongside Thurgood Marshall on the education cases assembled in some six or eight communities from the South to Kansas that became Brown, and Bob Carter as the quieter second attorney who meticulously built the South Carolina cases, leaving nothing unturned in work that could feel laborious to his researchers.

title: The Organizing Meeting Was a Woman
summary: Roxborough contrasts the clerical roles women held at headquarters with the branch presidencies they won, and names women central to the NAACP: Daisy E. Lampkin, who traveled organizing branches in the 1930s and 1940s; Althea T.L. Simmons, who headed the Washington Bureau after Clarence Mitchell; Constance Baker Motley in the legal defense fund; and Frances Hooks, who founded WIN, the women's structure within the NAACP.

title: A Female Brought the Report Back
summary: Roxborough notes the second female board chair and her successor Roslyn M. Brock, then recounts that the investigation of lynchings in Arkansas and Georgia behind the silent protest march in New York was carried out by a woman who brought the 1918 report to W.E.B. Du Bois, who received the publicity while the woman did the work that caused the 1919 march.

title: Send $5
summary: Asked about W.E.B. Du Bois, who predated her at the NAACP, Roxborough says she met him only briefly and once wrote for a copy of one of his papers, which he agreed to send for five dollars, and declines to repeat colleagues' stories about him rather than mislead the history.

title: Don't Hide Your Light Under a Bushel
summary: Roxborough resists naming a single proud accomplishment, explaining she worked full-time until 1997 and moved every five years into a new department, holding eight or ten jobs in nearly every part of the association except accounting, finding satisfaction in learning how the NAACP functioned and how she could make it work better.

title: An Education in Diplomacy and Self-Protection
summary: She describes the singular experience of traveling as a young unattached woman in the early 1950s, guarding against male-female complications and navigating branch presidents' authority, learning diplomacy and self-protection, then rising from field secretary through executive assistant, director of operations and programs, to development director, taking courses in the technical side of fundraising.

title: Walking the Children to School
summary: Roxborough names keeping the Hoxie, Arkansas parents inspired and walking their children to school as a source of satisfaction, and reflects that in danger, such as crossing cotton fields at night to local meetings under patrol, the fear came only afterward because there was no time to be afraid in the moment.

title: You're an Idiot If You Get Afraid
summary: She relays her parents' lesson that fear makes you your own worst enemy and recalls traveling some forty states over five years as a field secretary, including small Arkansas towns like El Dorado and Cotton Plant where a visitor from the New York NAACP was the worst thing the white power structure could hear of, with Mississippi, Georgia, and Alabama the most tense.

title: People Felt They Could Get Help
summary: Roxborough closes reflecting that staying in fearful families' homes and bringing them danger made her more humane and convinced her people are imperfect but worth any help, and that communities believed the NAACP would act on their behalf, placing on its leaders the responsibility to be effective and inspire confidence; the interview ends with thanks.
